item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the gimmick is neat, but I can't reasonably upvote an SCP that leaves me motion sick by the end. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hopefully not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Spent about five minutes trying to work out what everyone was on about. For anyone else equally baffled, actually try tryping something in the blank fields.

+1

(Which apparently makes me the 300th.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Didn't think it was working at first for me (using an Android phone), but it works just fine if you batter the return button hard enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy damn this was fun to read about. Loved the format screw though my poor keyboard is screaming in pain from how much I mashed it.

+1 for me. Currently my favourite infohazard SCP next to 2521.
As much as I love the format screw, there's nothing else here for me to love.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it would help if it gave more info, but as various paragraphs, and each load of the page loads a different paragraph that will appear with typing. That way you can get chunks of it at a time, learn more, develop it more, and not have the issue of it deleting too fast to make more description plausible. Heck, it could even be part of the containment procedure - perhaps they used a computer modeled on the human brain and have it cycle through the various sections so it can repair itself whenever the SCP starts deleting data, with it having a greater resistance to the SCP's effects due to being modeled somewhat on the one thing that can retain data on it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incredibly meta and also incredibly easy to upvote. Have my thumbs up, good coder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy fuck I actually started writing out an article at the beginning. I started with "SCP-2505 is" and by the time I had a second or two to start thinking of what to put next, it started deleting itself and I was DEFINITELY freaked out. I tried again but did a typo by accident and realized what was happening, but still SUPER interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A very well done and slow-burning horror. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that this works just the way it is. Upvoted for the creative format screw and the last line that had me bursting out into laughter. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the formatting is the article's greatest strength, it is also it's downfall. A SCP shouldn't be physically hard to read.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the formatting trick is extremely good, it's really all there is. The object in itself isn't very interesting and in my opinion not worthy of an upvote. The item number escapes me, but the pictogram SCP can pull off a format screw and still be an interesting article because the object itself is just as interesting as the method it's delivered. This isn't. It's a ball bearing you can't write stuff down about.

Not downvoting because of cool format trick, not upvoting either because of uninteresting object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like a copy for 055.

1-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is just boring to me. It's another uncreative format screw. I'm just not a fan of format screws that just do one thing and nothing else.

1-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WOW
just… WOW
How the hell do you guys come up with these things anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd love this a lot more if it wasn't essentially just 55, but I like format screws, especially clever ones, so I'll upvote.

I know it's old and there's probably zero chance of a rewrite at this point, but if it had more of an original idea behind the format screw it would be a lot better. For now it just feels like I'm reading about SCP-055 in a highly weird way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this was very cool, when I thought there would be something different for each Object Classification (with the base text being the Safe version), and then realized there was nothing else there (I even went through all of them to make sure). And the base text isn't very interesting.

Because of that, unfortunately, it's a -1 from me, though I admire the concept and am impressed with the Special Effect.

While I am a huge fan of format screw, but there's just not enough payoff for me as a reader compared to the effort I put in to read it; I had to try several times just to read the base text, let alone looking for complexity that wasn't there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this sentiment. But worse, it's a format screw that makes it hard to read through. Which kind of defeats the purpose.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow another scip that's totally boring outside of its format screw, don't have enough of those on the site yet. You can do a format screw better than this, Randomini.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn son, thats fancy.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Finally read this SCP.

It has a good gimmick, but that's all it is. All smoke and mirrors, no real substance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interactive element where the reader has to type along to get the full message of the SCP, though I wish that different object classes would deliver different Special Containment Procedures and Descriptions.

That said, it's a gimmick incarnate. A gimmick I can accept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: These format screws are becoming tiresome. Especially when they exist seemingly for the sole purpose of compensating for a weak concept. A fun house mirror version of a far better skip and oh look, it's a perfect sphere. How delightful. No amount of parsley and antique silverware can make a poorly cooked meal taste better. The same applies with fancy format gimmicks and a bland article. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If it'd been left alone, nothing, because no one would bother referring to it. If there was anything about its discovery and why anyone noticed its properties in the first place, I might be inclined to change my mind.

My point is that, since it does nothing else that any other of the countless ball bearings in the world can't do, why would anyone ever create a physical record of that particular ball bearing in the first place and then care when it disappeared? Like I said before, maybe an invoice or two got lost or altered. If it is a perfect sphere, obviously it was never used for its intended purpose, so no records of machines or facilities would be altered.

I want to know why the Foundation was alerted to its existence at all. I suppose that would count as "narrative," but without it, not only are the article and item uninteresting, the Foundation knowing about the item at all makes no sense. As it is, it's a gimmick with an item hammered into the shape of the gimmick with vague references to a popular article.

PS: I don't expect to convince anyone of my view. Opinions are subjective, and if you like it, more power to you. I just don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that this is an otherwise normal ball bearing is actually precisely why it would be a SCP. There is no good reason it should do this. The effect apparently can also spread to other words. The worst that could happen if it is left uncontained is a total loss of all of humanity's written records.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Vague spoilers?
– hide block

What do you think will happen once you start referring to SCP-2505, in-universe, with "it"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But the item really *isn't* interesting. It's a ball bearing. It does nothing. You just can't store information about it anywhere but the human brain.
Why anyone would have *bothered* storing information about an otherwise completely unremarkable ball bearing in the first place is beyond me. I guess there might have been an invoice or two that ended up missing a line item in the form of a case of ball bearings (or stating that there was one less bearing in the case than there actually was), but no one would have really taken note.
055 works because it might do something but we have no idea what, if anything, it does. With this, we know what it does, which is nothing, which is the very definition of uninteresting. It's an anomalous item at most and really doesn't deserve SCP status. If you left it lying in the street, the worst that would happen would be someone slipping on it like any other ball bearing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: David Rosen is Roget's go-to character. You can search his name with the search bar at the top right. Rosen appears in other works as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't work for me, I was on an Apple PC. :c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice format screw, I had lots of fun with this. +1
Also, not sure if this was intentional or not, but David Rosen is an actual person. He's the head programmer of the indie studio Wolfire Games. The man can code like no other. He pretty much single-handedly wrote the code for Overgrowth, which has some of the smartest combat AI I've ever seen. So props for that, if it was intentional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of torn; there's a lot of good ideas and I like the concept of the gimmick, but it also makes it bothersome to read once you get to where the new text is moving because the old text is disappearing. Is it possible to insert spaces instead of deleting the text so that it vanishes but doesn't shift the whole block?

Anyway, I think I'm neutral for the time being.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …my God. I didn't even realize that it was an anti SCP-055. This is a stroke of genius.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. A++, well done on being the first to get this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's actually a good theory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2505 is only to be referred to as SCP-2505. No pronouns are to be used to refer to SCP-2505.

But…why? There's nothing that even hints at something bad happening if pronouns are used.

EDIT: Holy crap I just got it.

Does that mean that...
Hide

the effect would spread to the pronoun, and it would start applying to the pronoun as well? Like, if I refer to SCP-2505 as "it", then anything referred to as "it" would delete itself after 7.4 seconds?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neutral vote; the gimmick is nifty, but the idea is either underdeveloped or lacking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was hard to read and lacking in concept. Generally, if the only thing it has going for it is the format screw, it's not a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. It's not often I say something like this, but this is fantastic.

At first, I thought this wasn't an SCP at all, but a template, so to speak, that people could fill out to easier make an SCP article. Then I started typing, saw the containment procedures appear, then was confused when they suddenly disappeared as I tried to read them.

I spent the next few minutes slamming the keyboard and trying to speed-read the text that resulted, then I got into the "SCP-2505 deletes data 7.4 seconds after it is created" bit and my brain EXPLODED.

+1, wish I could upvote twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holding one key down works just as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll give a neutral vote for now seeing as I can't type fast enough to read your SCP. Other than that I really like this gimmick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you just have a way of describing things mcpedro +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What in the world? This is so wacky and neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You need an excellent gimmick to cheat us out of a substantial item. This passes muster, no problem.

Also, from the sounds coming from my office, this is the most productive my colleagues think I have been in months.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that it's fair to say that what kills a lot of gimmick articles is that they don't go far enough. If you're gonna base it around a novelty concept, go whole-hog. Very rarely will you find a half story/half gimmick article that works. The only one I can think of is the Concept Stockbroker.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, seconding (thirding?) all that. The gimmick is very cool, but the article underneath it is just way to bare. I personally wouldn't mind hammering the keyboard for a while longer if it meant getting a more filled out story.

I have a rule about voting on format screws: imagine the article as a plain unobscured text, vote on that, then take the format screw into account to adjust the vote slightly. This one, without the gimmick, is a single non-particularly-interesting paragraph that would go into negatives fast. For me, having a really cool gimmick is not enough to outweight that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

The thing is, we have some other SCP (that I CBF to look up b/c I'm in class right now) which takes the concept of a "perfect sphere" to its logical end in a much more enjoyable way than this. The idea of something that cannot be understood in a strictly logical sense, and can only be recorded in a particular meaty, illogical storage system, is solid, but not as a perfect circle.


I'm inclined to agree with Kalinin, ultimately. This needs either expansion or a shift in concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I used mobile and it worked fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah I see. Yeah it does make it a little harder to read, I suppose.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, this is an article that's riding almost entirely on its gimmick. But you know what? It's a really, really clever gimmick. Sometimes that's enough. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is awesome!

Of course, I type faster than I can read, so I had to read it a lot of times, but it's still FREAKING AWESOME. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In some browsers/OS combinations, people claimed that didn't work. Furthermore, even if this method works, some people had trouble reading the content at that speed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, this is why I keep coming back and reading here. THIS. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An incredibly clever piece of formatting. Props to you on that Randomini. Wow. At first I thought that it was just a "create-your-own" scip like with SCP-[Even-Number]-J (which, by the way, clever tech support/-J message when one presses "Submit") and I was pleasantly surprised. Nice job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted for the format screw, but as soon as I started typing I immediately wanted to upvote again! :D

BEST. FORMAT SCREW. EVER!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It can also be spelled "memorise" with an "s." Often, Americans spell with z's what Europeans spell with s's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is possible to get a large portion of text by holding down one key continuously. You don't have to hammer a bunch of keys to get a scip that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Best format screw ever.

All these people complaining that there's "nothing else" are in fact the ones missing something. Forget narrative, I'm tired of it. Let's have some interesting items for once!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I don't mind format screws that much, it tends to be because the article itself is good enough to carry itself. Or the rare occasion in which the screw makes it more fun to read. Sadly this isn't the case here. Aside from the gimmick and the reference to another SCP (whose gimmick works much better) there is literally nothing to the article. Sure at the first glance the article seems interesting, but as soon as you find out what it does (which is midway through the description) it loses its appeal. Plus its an eye killer to read and a bit too slow. -1

I don't think there is anything wrong with the article itself, its just that some people will like the gimmick while others wont.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a gimmick but a fairly clever one! Think I'll upvote.
